meta.title: Sports Medicine Practice Software | Injury RTM & Return-to-Play Tracking | clinIQ
meta.description: Complete guide to sports medicine practice operations. $94K annual injury RTM. Systematic return-to-play protocols. Concussion management tracking. Musculoskeletal injection coordination.

hero.subhead: Athletes track their recovery obsessively — that monitoring is billable RTM. Return-to-play decisions need systematic documentation. Concussions need graded protocol tracking. clinIQ captures sports medicine RTM revenue, tracks return-to-play, and manages the full athlete care continuum.

sections.content:
Sports medicine practices treat a uniquely engaged patient population. Athletes are motivated — they want to recover faster, track their progress, and communicate with their providers between visits. That engagement is a revenue opportunity most sports medicine practices don't capture.

The average sports medicine practice has:

• 40-80 active injury patients at any time — each reporting symptoms, activity tolerance, and functional progress between visits
• Return-to-play decisions that require systematic protocol documentation but are managed informally
• Concussion cases requiring graded return protocols that carry significant medical-legal exposure if not documented properly
• Musculoskeletal injections that require authorization and coordination but lack systematic tracking
• High volume of school and professional athletes creating team coverage responsibilities

The monitoring athletes already do between visits — pain ratings, functional assessments, activity logging — qualifies for Remote Therapeutic Monitoring billing under CPT 98975-98981. Most practices collect this information informally through phone calls and portal messages. RTM makes it billable.

60 injury patients on RTM at $130/month = $7,800/month = $93,600/year. That's revenue for coordination work your team is already doing.

Remote Therapeutic Monitoring covers non-physiological data collection — exactly what sports medicine injury patients generate. Pain with activity, range of motion, functional capacity, PT compliance, and return-to-play progression all qualify.

**What sports medicine RTM monitoring includes:**

• Pain with activity: Visual analog scale, location, character, aggravating factors
• Range of motion self-assessment: Guided range testing compared to prior visit baselines
• Swelling and inflammation: Self-reported joint swelling, localized heat, skin changes
• Functional progress: Sport-specific movement quality, activity tolerance
• Physical therapy compliance: Session attendance, home exercise completion
• Activity restrictions: Compliance with activity modifications

**Sport-specific RTM templates:**

The monitoring protocol differs by injury type:

Acute musculoskeletal injuries (ankle sprain, shoulder impingement, knee sprain): Daily symptom tracking during acute phase, progressing to weekly during subacute.

Post-surgical sports injuries (ACL reconstruction, rotator cuff repair, labral repair): Extended monitoring through PT phases — acute, strengthening, return-to-sport.

Overuse injuries (stress fractures, tendinopathy, shin splints): Activity tolerance tracking guiding return to training load.

Chronic conditions (hip impingement, multi-directional instability): Ongoing symptom monitoring informing activity modification.

**The CPT codes:**

• 98975: RTM device supply and setup (one-time per episode)
• 98977: Monthly device supply with scheduled recordings
• 98980: Treatment management services, first 20 minutes
• 98981: Each additional 20 minutes (for complex patients)

**Enrollment and engagement:**

Athletes are the ideal RTM patients — highly motivated, technology comfortable, and invested in recovery tracking. Enrollment rates of 80-90% are typical when RTM is framed as part of the injury care program. Most athletes welcome structured recovery communication.

**Related: [Sports Medicine RTM: Injury Recovery Monitoring and Billing Guide](/blog/sports-medicine-rtm)**

Return-to-play decisions are among the highest-stakes clinical judgments in sports medicine. Clear the athlete too soon and you risk re-injury. Hold them out too long and you face pressure from coaches, athletes, and sometimes parents or agents. Without systematic documentation, return-to-play decisions carry significant medical-legal exposure.

**The return-to-play documentation problem:**

Most sports medicine practices manage return-to-play informally — a note in the chart when the athlete is cleared, but without documenting the protocol stages, objective criteria at each stage, and clinical rationale for progression. If an athlete re-injures after clearance, 'he seemed ready' is not adequate documentation.

**Return-to-play protocols by injury type:**

ACL reconstruction: 9-12 month protocol with specific functional criteria at each phase — range of motion milestones, strength symmetry testing, hop testing, sport-specific movement screening.

Concussion: Graduated return-to-play (GRTP) protocol with 6 stages — complete rest, light aerobic exercise, sport-specific exercise, non-contact drills, full-contact practice, return to competition. Each stage requires 24 hours minimum at that level before progression.

Ankle sprain: Functional progression from non-weight-bearing through sport-specific agility.

Rotator cuff repair: Phase-based protocol from passive range of motion through return to throwing.

**What clinIQ tracks:**

Protocol stage: Where the athlete is in their specific return-to-play protocol. Objective criteria: What must be achieved at this stage before progression — documented with measurements. Progression history: Date of each stage, criteria met, clinical rationale for progression. Clearance documentation: Final clearance date, conditions (graduated training load, protective equipment, etc.), provider signature. Communication: Automatic notification to athletic trainer or team physician when clearance is issued.

**Medical-legal protection:**

A timestamped, criterion-based return-to-play protocol in the permanent record is the standard of care. clinIQ creates that documentation automatically as the protocol progresses.

Concussion management in sports medicine involves a specific regulatory and medical-legal landscape. Most states have Return-to-Learn and Return-to-Play laws governing school athletes. Youth athletes require parent notification. Baseline testing creates comparison points. Graduated return protocols are the standard of care — and must be documented.

**The concussion management challenge:**

Athletes underreport symptoms — fear of missing playing time is the primary driver of concussion non-disclosure. Without systematic symptom tracking, underreporting goes undetected. Symptoms that appear resolved at a clinic visit may worsen with activity.

Return-to-Learn must precede Return-to-Play. Many sports medicine practices focus on Return-to-Play without coordinating with school for Return-to-Learn accommodations — a gap that creates medical-legal exposure.

**Graduated Return-to-Play Protocol stages:**

• Stage 1: Complete rest (24-48 hours minimum)
• Stage 2: Light aerobic activity — walking, swimming, stationary bike at <70% max heart rate
• Stage 3: Sport-specific exercise — running drills, skating drills — no head impact
• Stage 4: Non-contact training drills — passing drills, more complex movement
• Stage 5: Full-contact practice — medical clearance required before this stage
• Stage 6: Return to competition

Each stage requires minimum 24 hours symptom-free before progression. Any symptom recurrence returns to Stage 1.

**What clinIQ tracks:**

Symptom inventory: Standardized concussion symptom checklist — headache, pressure, nausea, dizziness, balance, visual changes, cognitive symptoms, sleep. Tracked with each contact.

GRTP stage: Current protocol stage, date of stage initiation, symptom status at stage entry.

Red flags: New or worsening symptoms trigger provider alert. Symptom during return exercise → document, step back, reassess.

School communication: Accommodation letter generation for academic adjustments during recovery.

Return-to-Learn coordination: Separate tracking for academic return alongside Return-to-Play.

Sports medicine practices perform a high volume of musculoskeletal injections — corticosteroid, platelet-rich plasma (PRP), hyaluronic acid, and prolotherapy. Each injection requires patient preparation, possibly ultrasound guidance, authorization for some biologics, and post-injection monitoring.

**Injection types and coordination requirements:**

Corticosteroid injections (knee, shoulder, hip, ankle): Generally require no prior authorization but have frequency limitations per payer (typically 3-4 per year per joint). Tracking prior injections per joint prevents authorization denials for exceeding frequency limits.

Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP): Many payers don't cover PRP — patient requires informed consent on self-pay pricing before scheduling. For workers' compensation patients, state-specific coverage rules apply.

Hyaluronic acid (knee OA): Medicare coverage with specific ICD-10 requirements. Prior authorization required by many commercial payers with documentation of failed conservative treatment.

Ultrasound-guided injections: Scheduling requires ultrasound machine availability and trained operator — either physician or MSK ultrasound-trained APP.

**What clinIQ coordinates:**

Injection scheduling: Right equipment, right room, right provider availability. Prior injection tracking: Joint-specific injection history prevents frequency-limit denials. Authorization tracking: HA injections requiring prior auth tracked from submission through approval. Post-injection monitoring: 15-30 minute monitoring after injection documented. Response tracking: Outcome of each injection informs future treatment decisions and prior auth documentation.

**Workers' compensation injection coordination:**

WC injections require claim number documentation, adjuster authorization (often separate from payer authorization), and specific reporting requirements. clinIQ tracks WC injection authorization alongside standard payer authorization.

Sports medicine practices with team coverage responsibilities manage a different operational layer — sideline coverage, event scheduling, athlete roster management, and injury reporting to coaching staff.

**Team coverage operational requirements:**

Event scheduling: Game and practice coverage calendars coordinated against clinic schedule. Coverage team — physician, PA, athletic trainer — confirmed for each event.

Injury reporting: Sideline injuries documented with mechanism, assessment, and disposition. Injuries requiring further evaluation tracked from sideline to clinic appointment.

Athletic trainer communication: Shared visibility into injury status, return-to-play protocol stage, and clearance decisions for athletes under joint care.

Roster management: Active athlete roster with current injury status visible for team physician decisions.

**School and professional team differences:**

High school team coverage: HIPAA and minor consent considerations. Parent communication required for evaluation and treatment decisions. Return-to-play laws vary by state — most require physician or healthcare provider clearance.

College athletes: NCAA regulations govern return-to-play timelines for specific injuries and procedures. Concussion return-to-play must be physician-cleared.

Professional athletes: Employer health information considerations. Negotiated contract provisions may affect treatment decisions.

**What clinIQ manages:**

Event coverage calendar: Upcoming games and practices with coverage assignments. Injury log per team: All injuries documented with dates, mechanism, assessment, and current status. Athletic trainer dashboard: Current injury status for shared patients visible to both clinic and athletic training staff. Communication templates: Clearance letters, restriction letters, and school accommodation letters generated from patient record.

**Week 1-2: Configuration**

RTM program built for your patient population — injury types, sport-specific templates, billing thresholds. Return-to-play protocols configured for your most common injuries. Concussion management workflow built to your protocol. Injection tracking configured with your payer mix authorization requirements.

**Week 3: Training and Go-Live**

Provider training on RTM review dashboard and return-to-play tracking. MA and front desk training on check-in integration. Athletic trainer communication workflow set up if applicable.

**ROI Timeline:**

Month 1: RTM enrollment begins. First billing cycle initiated. Return-to-play documentation starting.

Month 3: 50+ injury patients enrolled. First full quarter RTM billing showing $23,000+. Authorization denial rate dropping for HA injections.

Month 6: Steady-state RTM at $7,800+/month. Concussion protocol documentation complete across all active cases. Athletic trainer coordination established.

**Annual ROI:**

• Injury RTM revenue: $93,600/year (60 patients)
• Authorization denial reduction: Practice-specific — typically $15,000-30,000/year
• Return-to-play documentation risk reduction: Incalculable but significant medical-legal protection

**Investment:**

• Professional: $499/month — Full suite including RTM, return-to-play tracking, concussion management, injection coordination
• Implementation: $750 one-time

faqs.q: Is injury RTM covered by insurance?
faqs.a: Yes. Medicare and most commercial payers cover musculoskeletal RTM under CPT 98975, 98977, 98980, and 98981. Injury recovery monitoring — pain with activity, range of motion, functional progress, PT compliance — qualifies as non-physiological remote monitoring. Reimbursement averages $120-150 per patient per month depending on payer. Workers' comp RTM billing follows state-specific schedules.

faqs.q: How do return-to-play protocols work in clinIQ?
faqs.a: Each injury type has a configured protocol with specific stages and objective criteria at each stage. The athlete's current stage, criteria met, and progression history are all documented. Final clearance is timestamped and attributed to the clearing provider. The system generates clearance documentation for schools, coaches, and athletic trainers.

faqs.q: How do you handle concussion management documentation?
faqs.a: clinIQ implements the Graduated Return-to-Play Protocol with all 6 stages. Each stage requires symptom-free confirmation before progression. Symptom tracking occurs between each stage. Red flags trigger provider notification. Return-to-Learn accommodations and school communication are documented separately. All documentation is timestamped and creates a complete medical-legal record.

faqs.q: Can athletic trainers access the system?
faqs.a: Yes. Shared patient dashboards give athletic trainers visibility into injury status, return-to-play protocol stage, and current restrictions for athletes under joint care. Communication between clinic and athletic training staff happens within the system with documentation.

faqs.q: How does injection tracking prevent authorization denials?
faqs.a: clinIQ tracks injections by joint with dates and CPT codes. When a new injection is scheduled, frequency history is visible — preventing scheduling injections that would exceed payer frequency limits. For procedures requiring prior auth (hyaluronic acid, some PRP), authorization status is tracked from submission through approval before scheduling.

faqs.q: How long until we see RTM revenue?
faqs.a: RTM billing begins in month 1 after enrollment. Most practices see first checks at 30-45 days. By month 3 with 50+ enrolled injury patients, monthly RTM revenue is $6,000-7,500. By month 6 at full enrollment, most practices achieve the $7,800+/month benchmark. Athletes are among the most compliant RTM patients — engagement rates above 85% are typical.

faqs.q: Do you work with both clinic-based and sideline sports medicine practices?
faqs.a: Yes. Clinic-based configuration focuses on injury RTM, return-to-play tracking, and injection coordination. Practices with team coverage add event scheduling, injury reporting, and athletic trainer communication. Both workflows are supported within the same system.

faqs.q: How does workers' compensation RTM billing work?
faqs.a: Workers' compensation RTM follows state-specific fee schedules and requires claim number documentation. clinIQ tracks WC injury patients separately with claim-specific billing information. Authorization for treatment (including RTM) follows WC adjuster approval workflows. Post-treatment reporting requirements are documented within the patient record.

testimonial.quote: Athletes love tracking their recovery. Enrollment is easy because they want to report progress. We went from $0 in RTM to $8,200 per month in four months.
